channel.title: Seasonal Observatory
channel.description: An English edition of Seasonal Observatory: edited guides to China's twenty-four solar terms, urban phenology, indoor light, humidity, food, and small repeatable observations.

item.title: Minor Heat in the City: How One Street Learns Summer First
item.description: Xiaoshu, Minor Heat, is more than a date on a calendar. In a city it appears in warm pavement, shortened shade, loud air-conditioners, and clouds gathering after dusk.

item.title: Grain in Ear and the Rain Line: Crops, Damp Rooms, and City Drains
item.description: Mangzhong is often introduced through farming, but city readers can find it in rain lines, mildew smells, swollen greenery, and the slow work of drainage after a storm.

item.title: Beginning of Autumn Is Not Cool: Where the First Turn Appears
item.description: Liqiu often arrives while the weather is still hot. Its value is not instant coolness but a change in attention: light, night wind, leaf edges, and market shelves begin to shift.

item.title: White Dew on the Balcony: Tiny Evidence of Night Cooling
item.description: Bailu can be read in a drop of water, but only if we keep the observation precise: surface, hour, material, weather, and whether the water is truly dew.

item.title: Cold Dew at Night: Wind, Lamplight, and the First Raised Collar
item.description: Hanlu does not need dramatic cold. It often appears on the way home, where night wind, clearer air, warm shopfronts, and the edge of a sleeve make autumn unmistakable.

item.title: Winter Solstice Light: How the Shortest Day Enters a Desk
item.description: Dongzhi can begin with astronomy, but it becomes intimate when afternoon light leaves a room early and the desk lamp becomes part of the day's structure.
